sentences.text: My name is Edith Millman.
E-D-I-T-H M-I-L-L-M-A-N.
Greifinger.
G-R-E-I-F-I-N-G-E-R.
Yes.
I was born May 30, 1924.
I was born in Bielsko, Poland.
Yes.
I lived there until I was almost 13.
And in 1937, we moved to Warsaw.
Well, I didn't have any brothers or sisters.
It was just my parents and me.
My-- my grandparents lived nearby, my mother's parents.
She was one of five sisters.
And my father's family lived in Galicia.
And he was one of eight siblings.
Yes.
My last name was Elfriede, and her maiden name was Posner.
Was Osias.
O-S-I-A-S.
Well, I-- it was a good life-- upper middle class.
My father was an executive for an American oil company.
He-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the first World War, he studied law in Vienna.
And in 1920, he was hired by the American oil company.
At that time it was Vacuum Oil and they changed to Mobil Oil and changed to Standard Oil.
But he knew English and German and Polish.
And so he got the good position, and he worked his way up to an executive position.
So my family was upper middle class.
We had a live-in maid, and I spent my summers at the seashore.
I loved to swim.
And winters in in the mountains.
We lived near the mountains, near the Carpathian Mountains.
And so I was an-- kind of an expert swimmer.
My parents were extremely active in all kinds of charitable organizations and all kinds of communal activities, both non-Jewish and Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a child, I was a Zionist and belonged to a Zionist youth group.
My life was a very happy life although my father was a strict disciplinarian.
He was extremely strict with me.
So I wasn't really-- and I wasn't spoiled like you would think that the only child of an upper middle class family would be spoiled.
But I-- I was not.
My folks just didn't spoil me.
What were the names of the organizations that your parents were active in?
Well, I don't remember.
There were all kinds of philanthropic organization.
I know my mother was--
Both.
Jewish and non-Jewish.
I do know, remember vaguely, my mother was, I think, active in art.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember.
Akiba, and actually it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] more religious Zionist organization.
And I didn't have a religious upbringing.
But my friends belonged to this organization.
That's how I joined.
Yes, but they were not observant.
Although my grandparents were very Orthodox, my parents were not.
We didn't have a kosher home.
We just went to the synagogue on the high holy days, on Yom Kippur and on Rosh Hashanah The Seders were held at my grandparents' place.
The kind of strange thing is that my mother's grandfather was a Huguenot.
It was a Huguenot family that escaped from France during the persecution.
And the family changed religion.
And then the-- the son-- one of the sons married the Jewish girl, and they became very Orthodox.
And my grandmother was extremely Orthodox with this Huguenot grandfather, I mean, Huguenot father, really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father's family was-- was a very, very Orthodox-- his parents were.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The children of my aunts and uncles I don't think were too Orthodox anymore.
It was a, what we would say here, a conservative synagogue.
Well, it was mixed.
We had some non-Jewish friends.
My father, I know, had one friend that I am going to talk about him a little later.
And he was a strong, Polish patriot.
And, but he-- he used to spend many evenings with my father playing chess.
I had some non-Jewish friends and some Jewish friends.

Although when I walked to school in elementary school, there were-- sometimes some young kids would yell, 'Dirty Jew!' And I do know that whenever there was a soccer game, when it emptied, when-- after the soccer game, we didn't go into the street.
We were afraid because the-- all the-- somehow the soccer fans were always very rambunctious and noisy.
And somehow they always started with anti-Semitic remarks or yelling.
And also, at Easter time when we-- we were usually afraid because people coming out from the church frequently didn't like-- you know, said something against the Jews.
But in my early years, I didn't hear too much, didn't have too much anti-Semitism.
I did attend first a public school where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were prayers every morning, and the Jewish kids just had to stand.
And we didn't take part in prayers when the priest came.
They would have a-- at the same time, they would have a teacher come and teach us Jewish history, more modern religion.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father hired a halutz.
It's a young man who was preparing to go to Palestine.
He was in the Hachsharah.
And he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he taught me Hebrew as a living language.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So later on, I-- and-- my father-- from fourth grade on, I went to a private Jewish school.
But the schools were-- girls went to school separately.
It was-- it was segregated.
Boys and girls didn't go to the same class and didn't attend the same class.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then you move to Warsaw when you were 13?
Yes.
In 1947 my father was transferred to Warsaw.
It was a promotion for him.
And we lived in a very beautiful new apartment building.
Matter of fact, when we moved in, I think we were the second or third tenants.
It was so new.
It was very modern and in a-- in center city.
And now, my-- since my father was afraid that I might not know how to handle a big city, I went to Catholic, a parochial school for my second year of high school.
The first year of high school, I had done in Bielsko.
But the second year, it was a Catholic school though there were about, I would say, 30%, 35%, maybe 40% Jewish kids in that class.
We were taught by nuns.
And for one year, I attended that Catholic school.
And then as I got more used to life in a big city, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my folks transferred me to a private Jewish high school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No not really.
Not really.
We got along just fine.
So we didn't happen have any problems.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, sure.
My folks were very aware.
They were always listening to the radio and the news and always discussion about the political situation.
My father was a-- a born pessimist, and he really was afraid that a war will break out.
And matter of fact, I think already 19- late '38 or maybe beginning of '39, he took the money that they had saved out of the bank.
And he bought some American dollars and some gold coins because he felt that the situation was just dangerous.
There was anti-Semitism [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time.
Well, actually, it started earlier from almost the time that Hitler came to power.
There was more anti-Semitism in Poland.
Too, the ideology really spread.
And there was a right-wing party, so-called Endecy, E-N-D-E-C-Y, who were very anti-Semitic, very right-wing.
And they frequently went into the Jewish part of Warsaw and beat some Jews up.
There was a quota system at the universities.
And Jewish students had to either stand or sit in the back-- I think mostly stand.
And there were fights.
After lectures when they would come out of the university, there were fights between-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --Jewish students and non-Jewish students.
By anti-Semitism?
Well, you know, we--we-- we were angry about it.
But I, personally, didn't have too much experience with it.
But we always heard about people being attacked by hoodlums.
And we knew about the arrests at the university-- at the university.
Yes.
Well, it made me feel that I do want to go to Palestine and that I didn't-- you know, I wasn't welcome.
That's why I guess why I was a Zionist, and I always looked up-- we'd heard all these wonderful things about what the Zionists were doing in Palestine or the halutzim and the kibbutzim.
And so we were full of admiration for them.
And we were collecting money-- even, I remember in, gee, in elementary school we had little stamps that we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] buying for, I don't know, $0.10 or the equivalent of $0.10.
And then we would put them in a book.
And when we had a filled book, we would bring in the money with the stamps.
And we were very proud, you know, when we could fill up a book and collect, like, $5 or whatever.
That-- that was always going for Keren Kayemeth and for Palestine.
Well, my father registered.
He really wanted to go to America, and he registered for immigration in 1938, I think.
But there was this strict quota system.
And in 19-- I think his number was supposed to come up in 1941 or '42.
And there were-- people were leaving, kind of, illegally to different countries.
But first of all, it did cost a lot of money.
And my father was very, up and very upright person, and he wouldn't do anything that wasn't very legal.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so, so we were just waiting for the quota to come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, I was in Warsaw.
And the war fever really was quite strong at that time, you know, after the-- Czechoslovakia was evaded-- invaded, and after Austria was invaded.
Poland really knew that they were going to be next.
And that's when the anti-German feelings was being stirred up.

And all the loudspeakers and announcements on the walls of buildings proclaim '[NON-ENGLISH],' which means, 'strong, united, and ready.' But unfortunately, Poland was neither strong, not really united, nor-- nor ready.
And then when the Germans invaded, it was such a complete surprise.
I mean, all of Poland expected it, but there was-- I don't think there was any declaration of war.
There were just all the unrest in Gdansk, which is Danzig, because the Germans wanted that part of Poland.
And that's where the trouble really started.
And also in Silesia-- that's the part where I was born-- there were many, many Germans.
And they also were for Hitler.
And there were fights then between the Poles and the-- and the Germans.
By the way, that's because there were so-- this part of Poland belonged to Germany before the First World War.
And so my mother had a-- a German education.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and we spoke mostly German at home.
At least, I was bilingual.
We spoke both languages.
And that helped me during the war.
Naturally, that doesn't mean that everybody who knew German had an easier time, but in my case, it really helped quite a bit.

Well, and just before the war broke out, there were announcements on the radios and loudspeakers that every apartment had to have one room that is airtight because, you know, gas was used in the-- during the first World War, so they expected gas to be used during this war.
So everybody had to make one room airtight.
And I remember my mother taping windows in one room.
And then she decided that the bathroom would be a better room because there were no windows.
And she was taping at the doors and whatnot.
And we were told to prepare a-- a-- a first aid kit.
Everybody should have a first aid kit and that we had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gas masks.
We were buying-- people were buying gas masks, all preparing for gas.
And there were drills, like air raid drills so-- where we were told to go to-- but there were no shelters but like basements or sub-basements.
And when the sirens went off, everybody was practicing how to get to shelters.
And-- but then when the war broke out, it was so sudden we didn't even know if it was a-- a-- the real air-- the drill or the real air raid.
And it was-- it turned out it was the real air raid.
I was in Warsaw in 1939, and the bombardment started almost immediately.
And there were incendiary bombs falling all over.
And Warsaw was just in flame.
I don't know what the percentage of the city was that was destroyed, but I think at least about 35%, 40% was destroyed by-- in the first onslaught.
People were taken out of the of Warsaw to dig ditches, anti-tank ditches.
Labor battalions were organized, and people were digging ditches.
Then the artillery was coming closer and closer, and we were being shelled.
On ninth in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think on September 6 or so, an order was given for all able-bodied men, the age between, I think, 15 and 55 or 60 should leave Warsaw and go east.
Warsaw is on a-- at the Vistula River.
So that meant crossing the river and going east towards Russia.
And I understand that the idea was that then Warsaw could be declared, then like, an open city-- that there was no-- no men and only women and children.
So supposedly, Hitler was not supposed to bomb it and-- because it was an open city, an undefended city.
But it was incongruous because on the other hand they did prepare these ditches for, you know, for defense.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father took his knapsack and left with thousands of other people.
And matter of fact, he was sick before he left.
And we had the doctor there day or two before.
And he said my father should have a lot of rest.
He had some chest pain, and we didn't know if it was a light heart attack or something.
But anyway, he did leave-- and in the middle of the night.
And as these-- all these thousands of men were leaving, the German airplanes came swoop down very low and started to machine gun these people.
And we heard the planes, and we heard the noise, and you know.
And then some people came running back saying that they are shooting, machine gunning these people because the roads were just clogged.
There were just a few bridges over the Vistula River and a few highways.
So there were thousands of people on there.
And we had no idea if my father had survived or not.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As it turned out-- it's an aside-- he did survive.
And he and two other men got together, put some of their money, and bought a horse and a wagon because they wanted-- were tired carrying their knapsacks.
And they were hoping to, you know, ride, go on the wagon.
But they didn't know much about horses.
And it seems that they got a lame horse, and it was half blind.
And it was very decrepit.
And they-- and so they felt sorry for the horse.
So in the beginning, they put their knapsacks on.
Then they felt sorry for the horse.
And they unhitched the wagon and left it.
But they knew that the horse couldn't manage by itself.
So they were still-- they were pulling the horse.
And wherever they could, they bought some hay or something for it.
And then it was getting too, too much.
So finally they found a farmer who took the horse from them.
But they had to pay the farmer to take the horse.
And the farmer promised that he's going to feed the horse.
My father walked all the way till he met with the advancing Soviet army because Hitler made the pact with Stalin before the war to divide Poland.
And as he met the Soviet army, he didn't like what he saw.
And he certainly didn't want to be under the Soviets.
So he turned around and was coming back.
On the way, he got extremely sick.
And somebody had seen him when he was so sick, and they came and-- and told us that they had seen my father but that he had survived the original shooting but that he was very sick.
So we still didn't know if he lived or not.
But he did come back later after the German-- the Germans occupied Warsaw.
Well, during the bombardment, my-- we lived on-- well, we lived on the fifth floor, and the electricity was out so thereon the elevator didn't work anymore.
And my mother had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had the keys to an apartment in-- on the first floor.
And she thought-- it belonged to a friend of hers who had left.

And so she decided to take some of our more valuable stuff-- like the winter coats and some food that she had because she used to can fruit and, I don't remember, vegetables, but mostly fruit like cherries and like applesauce.
And so she-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she was taking that stuff down.
I was taking it down from the fifth floor to the-- to the first floor.
And she was arranging them, all that stuff, in the bathroom, which was all tiled and--
OK.
